intro:
Coffee Meets Bagel built a reputation for quality-over-quantity dating: a limited number of curated matches per day, a women-first design where women receive and review matches before men can message, and a general philosophy against the endless swipe.

Embir shares the quality-over-quantity instinct but takes it further: verified profiles, human moderation, full LGBTQ+ inclusion, deep compatibility matching across orientations, and a free-at-launch model that doesn't require a subscription to access meaningful features.

comparisonTable.embir: Universal — all orientations from day one

sections.title: Quality matching — a shared philosophy, different execution
sections.content:
CMB and Embir agree on the core problem with swipe culture: volume without context produces shallow interactions. CMB addresses this with daily match limits. Embir addresses it differently: deeper compatibility signals at signup mean every profile you see already meets your stated criteria — no need to artificially limit daily views.

CMB's bean currency for premium actions (opening 'Woo', viewing activity reports, unlocking additional matches) adds friction in ways that feel more like monetization than curation. Embir's free launch phase removes this friction entirely.
highlights: Compatibility filtering means every match already fits your criteria
No artificial daily limits that push you toward premium upgrades
Orientation, intentions, and values are core matching signals

sections.title: LGBTQ+ users — an afterthought vs a priority
sections.content:
CMB's design was built around heterosexual dating patterns — the women-message-first model assumes a binary gender dynamic. Same-sex matching is available but clearly sits outside the core product design. LGBTQ+ users report a noticeably thinner experience.

Embir was designed without gender assumptions baked in. Every orientation and gender identity has the same matching experience. There's no 'default' dating dynamic that other users work around.
highlights: No binary gender assumptions in the matching flow
Gay, lesbian, bi, pan, trans, non-binary — all fully supported
Orientation preferences are central to the algorithm, not an overlay

verdict:
Coffee Meets Bagel is a good fit if you're in the US, identify as a heterosexual woman, and want a slow-paced curated experience with some social accountability via Facebook linking.

Embir is the better option if you want verified profiles, full LGBTQ+ support, compatibility-first matching, free access at launch, and human moderation — across France, UK, and the US.
ctaText: Quality connections, no beans required — join free

faq.q: Is Embir like Coffee Meets Bagel in terms of quality over quantity?
faq.a: Yes — Embir shares the philosophy. But instead of daily limits, Embir uses deep compatibility matching so every profile you see already meets your criteria. Less noise from the start.
Core features are free during launch. No beans, no in-app currency, no subscription required to message.

faq.q: Does Embir work for LGBTQ+ users?
faq.a: Yes. Embir was built for every orientation from day one — gay, lesbian, bi, pan, queer, trans, non-binary. All identities have the same full matching experience.
Selfie verification from signup, plus human moderation of every report. The founding community scale makes genuine oversight feasible.

faq.q: Is Embir available in the US?
faq.a: Yes. Embir is launching in the US, France, and UK. Founding members in the US can join now.
